title: How to Build a Lead Priority Queue in 60 Minutes
tldr: Grade every open lead A–F by Fit/Intent/Quality, rank by composite score (grade + overdue + recency), work top-down every morning. Start manual in Google Sheets; automate when you pass 200 leads.
why: Without a Priority Queue, your reps burn 30–90 minutes every morning deciding which lead to call first. That's ₹2–5 lakh per month in rep-hours wasted on triage, and 40–70% of Grade A leads going cold because they weren't called in the window. A priority system is the single highest-leverage behaviour change for most Indian B2B SMBs.
prerequisites: Access to your current lead list (CRM, Google Sheet, or CSV)
A rough ICP definition — even 3 attributes is enough (industry, geography, company size)
15 minutes of rep-shadow time to confirm current workflow

steps.heading: Export your open leads
steps.body: Pull every open lead from your existing system into a Google Sheet. Columns: name, phone, email, source, date added, last touch, stage.

steps.heading: Score each lead
steps.body: Add three columns: Fit (0–100), Intent (0–100), Quality (0–100). Score each lead by hand. Fit: ICP match. Intent: engagement recency. Quality: data completeness. 50 leads takes ~90 minutes the first time.

steps.heading: Assign grades
steps.body: Grade A: F≥65 + I≥60 + Q≥60. Grade B: F≥55 + I≥40 + Q≥50. Grade C: F≥40 + I≥25 + Q≥35. Grade F: Q<20. Add a Grade column.

steps.heading: Add rank score
steps.body: Formula: grade_points + overdue_bonus + recency_bonus. Grade weights: A=4000, B=3000, C=2000, D=500, F=-1. Overdue bonus: 800 if past SLA. Recency: up to 500.

steps.heading: Sort and distribute
steps.body: Sort by rank score descending. Top 30 per rep is the day's work. Share the sheet with reps Monday morning.

steps.heading: Measure for 2 weeks
steps.body: Track: % of Grade A contacted within SLA, ₹ recovered from previously-stale leads, Grade A conversion rate. Most teams recover ₹1–3 lakh in the first two weeks.

steps.heading: Automate when manual breaks
steps.body: Past 200 leads, nobody updates the rank column religiously. Leadkaun automates this in 60 minutes of onboarding — same formula, no spreadsheet maintenance.

commonMistakes: Sorting by recency instead of grade — defeats the whole point
Not decaying Intent over time — queue fills with stale Grade A leads
Working Grade F (junk) because they're 'easier to call' — burns time

faqs.q: How long before I see impact?
faqs.a: 2 weeks for measurable ₹ recovered; 60 days for cultural shift to 'queue-first' mornings.

faqs.q: What if my reps resist?
faqs.a: Don't mandate on day 1. Show the top rep's queue vs their gut-choices for a week. Data wins the argument.

faqs.q: Do I need a new CRM?
faqs.a: No. Start in Google Sheets. Automate only when manual breaks (usually around 200 leads or 3+ reps).

title: How to Set Up Lead Scoring for Your Sales Team
tldr: Define three scores (Fit, Intent, Quality), each 0–100, with transparent weights. Assign grades A–F using a fixed matrix. Start with Leadkaun defaults; customise per ICP only after 30 days of data.
why: Lead scoring is the decision layer on top of your pipeline — it tells your rep who to call first. Done right, it converts Grade A leads 3–5× better than ungraded pipelines. Done wrong (single-number black-box AI scores), reps don't trust the output and revert to gut feel.
prerequisites: A rough ICP — at minimum: target industry, geography, company size
30 days of historical closed-won deal data (or industry benchmarks if new)
Rep consensus on 3–5 intent signals that matter in your workflow

steps.heading: Define Fit weights
steps.body: Total 100 points across: industry (30), geography (20), business type (20), role (15), budget (15). Adjust within your team — a real estate team might weight geography higher.

steps.heading: Define Intent signals + weights
steps.body: Source baseline (referral 60, organic 50, paid ad 30). Call signal +15. WhatsApp reply +10. Meeting booked +25. Intent decay −3/day after threshold.

steps.heading: Define Quality gate
steps.body: Valid phone (30), email (15), company name (15), inquiry clarity (20), source reliability (10), junk penalty (up to −10). Quality < 20 = auto-Grade F.

steps.heading: Define grade matrix
steps.body: Grade A: F≥65 + I≥60 + Q≥60. Grade B: F≥55 + I≥40 + Q≥50. Grade C: F≥40 + I≥25 + Q≥35. Grade D: F≥20 + I≥10 + Q≥20. Grade F: Q<20.

steps.heading: Apply to existing leads
steps.body: Score every open lead by hand initially. Verify distribution: ~15% A, ~25% B, ~35% C, ~15% D, ~10% F is a healthy shape. If you're 50% A, your Fit weights are too lenient.

steps.heading: Communicate grades to reps
steps.body: Every rep should know what Grade A means and the SLA (24 hours). Post the grade matrix in the sales room.

steps.heading: Refine after 30 days
steps.body: Pull conversion data by grade. If Grade A converts <15%, weights need tightening. If Grade C converts >8%, weights are too strict.

commonMistakes: Using a single black-box AI score — reps don't trust what they can't explain
Setting Intent without decay — queue fills with permanently-stale Grade A leads
Tuning weights before seeing 30 days of data

faqs.q: Can I skip this and use Leadkaun's defaults?
faqs.a: Yes. Defaults are calibrated for Indian B2B SMBs and work for ~80% of teams on day one.

faqs.q: How often should I tune weights?
faqs.a: Every 90 days, based on conversion data per grade. Don't tune more often — you'll chase noise.

faqs.q: Different weights per product line?
faqs.a: Yes on the Growth tier. Multi-product teams typically have 2–4 ICP profiles.

title: How to Run a Daily Morning Brief for Your Sales Team
tldr: Send each rep and manager a daily 8:30 AM IST email listing top Grade A leads, ₹ at risk today, and overdue follow-ups. Set the day before stand-up. Teams that adopt the ritual move 2–5× faster on Grade A leads.
why: Morning Brief is the ritual that turns lead scoring and priority queues into a daily habit. A rep who opens their inbox to '₹1.8L to recover today — Priya, Rajesh, Mohan' has their day planned before coffee. A manager who opens their inbox to a per-rep rollup has the 1:1 already written. No dashboards opened, no stand-up debates — just action by 10 AM.
prerequisites: A graded lead system (hand-graded or via Leadkaun)
Each rep has an assigned lead pool
Email delivery infrastructure (Resend, SendGrid, or your CRM's email)

steps.heading: Define the rep brief structure
steps.body: Four sections: (1) Top 5 Grade A leads needing callback today, (2) ₹ at risk today, (3) Overdue follow-ups, (4) One-line reminder of the SLA.

steps.heading: Define the manager brief structure
steps.body: Per-rep rollup: ₹ at risk per rep, Grade A count per rep, overdue count per rep. One team total line at the top.

steps.heading: Compute ₹ at risk per lead
steps.body: Formula: avg deal value for grade × grade conversion rate. For a Grade A real-estate lead: ₹45L × 10% = ₹4.5L.

steps.heading: Schedule delivery at 8:30 AM IST
steps.body: Crontab: 0 3 * * 1-6 (UTC 3 AM = IST 8:30 AM, Monday–Saturday). Use Inngest or equivalent background job scheduler.

steps.heading: Frame copy as opportunity, not blame
steps.body: Rep version: '₹1.8L to recover today' not 'You missed ₹1.8L'. Manager version: per-rep numbers without commentary.

steps.heading: Include one-tap action links
steps.body: Every lead row has a 'Call now' link that opens the lead record. Reduces friction to zero.

steps.heading: Measure adoption for 2 weeks
steps.body: Track: email open rate (target 80%+), click-through rate to lead records (target 60%+), Grade A contact time shift (should drop 30–50%).

commonMistakes: Sending at 9 AM or later — misses the pre-standup window
Including too much data (more than 5 leads) — reps skim and miss signals
Using blame language in rep copy — reps tune out

faqs.q: Does Leadkaun send this automatically?
faqs.a: Yes. Morning Brief is a core feature — 8:30 AM IST, Monday–Saturday, every tier.

faqs.q: What if a rep is on leave?
faqs.a: Toggle 'out of office' in settings; Brief pauses for them.

faqs.q: Can I customise what managers see?
faqs.a: Yes — pick which per-rep metrics appear (₹ at risk, Grade A count, follow-up score, etc.).

title: How to Import 10,000 Leads from CSV Cleanly
tldr: Clean the CSV first (phone normalisation, duplicate detection, quality checks), then upload. Leadkaun handles 10,000 rows in under 60 seconds. Expect 20–40% of historical CSV data to be junk — that's normal.
why: CSV imports are where most 'CRM migrations' fail. Teams upload raw files, skip cleaning, then blame the tool when grading looks wrong. A 30-minute pre-upload audit doubles the quality of everything downstream — Fit scores, grade distributions, rep time spent.
prerequisites: Source CSV with at least: name, phone, email, source, date added
30 minutes for cleaning and validation
Access to your Leadkaun or target system

steps.heading: Dedupe on phone + name
steps.body: Indian phone formats vary (+91, leading 0, with spaces). Normalise to E.164 before dedup. Expect to drop 5–15% of rows.

steps.heading: Validate phone format
steps.body: Any row without a 10-digit Indian number (after normalisation) goes to a 'quarantine' sheet for manual review — usually junk or international.

steps.heading: Check company name sanity
steps.body: Filter for test strings ('asdf', 'test', 'xxx'). Flag rows without company name for manual ICP tagging.

steps.heading: Audit source reliability
steps.body: If 40%+ of a source's leads are junk, that source will bring down overall data quality. Consider excluding it from the import until source is fixed.

steps.heading: Upload via Leadkaun CSV import
steps.body: Drag-drop into the import UI. Leadkaun streams via Supabase Storage + Inngest batch worker (BATCH_SIZE=500). 10k rows finish in under 60 seconds.

steps.heading: Review Import Insight Screen
steps.body: Shows distribution by grade, source, and quality. If 60% are Grade F, the source data has a quality problem — don't blame the tool.

steps.heading: Run first Morning Brief on day 2
steps.body: Day 1 scoring is stabilising. Day 2 Morning Brief is the first real signal — check with reps whether the Grade A list looks right.

commonMistakes: Skipping the phone normalisation step — 15–25% of leads become duplicates
Uploading without sanity-checking source reliability
Panicking when grade distribution looks unexpected — often reflects real data quality, not tool error

faqs.q: Can I undo an import?
faqs.a: Yes. Bulk-delete from the lead list with a date-added filter.

faqs.q: Does import overwrite existing data?
faqs.a: Dedup on phone — existing records get merged with new source tag logged.

faqs.q: Large imports hang Google Sheets?
faqs.a: For 50k+ row files, split into 10k-row chunks or use CSV directly.

title: How to Migrate from Zoho CRM to Leadkaun (in 2–4 Weeks)
tldr: Export from Zoho as CSV, audit (keep only live leads), onboard Leadkaun (60 min), run parallel for 30 days, cut over, maintain Zoho read-only as archive. Total: 2–4 weeks for an SMB.
why: Most Indian SMBs leave Zoho because of rep-adoption failure, complexity, or the realisation that 'workflow CRM' isn't what they need — they need a Sales Behaviour OS. The migration itself is not the hard part; psychology is. Most teams think 'downgrading' when they should think 'rightsizing'.
prerequisites: Zoho admin access (export permission)
Clear business case: what ROI do you expect from Leadkaun
2–4 weeks of parallel-running bandwidth

steps.heading: Week 1: Export Zoho data
steps.body: Export Leads, Contacts, Accounts, Deals, Activity History as CSVs. Activity History is the big one — it carries every call, email, note.

steps.heading: Week 1: Audit
steps.body: Separate live leads (last 90 days activity) from archive. Most SMBs find 40–60% of Zoho data is stale. Migrate only live data.

steps.heading: Week 2: Onboard Leadkaun
steps.body: 60-minute wizard. Pick industry (pre-configured ICP templates), import live leads via CSV, configure sales stages, create rep accounts.

steps.heading: Week 2: Parallel-run
steps.body: Keep Zoho running in read-only mode for safety. Reps use Leadkaun primarily; critical escalations can reference Zoho.

steps.heading: Week 3: Rep training + measurement
steps.body: 2-hour session per team. Show 3-tap logging, Priority Queue, Morning Brief. Baseline metrics: rep adoption rate, response time, ₹ recovered.

steps.heading: Week 4: Cutover
steps.body: Switch primary system. Maintain Zoho read-only for 90 days as fallback archive.

steps.heading: Day 90: Cancel Zoho
steps.body: If the 30-day parallel-run numbers hold (rep adoption >60%, response time halved, ₹ recovered > licence cost), cancel Zoho. Archive CSV export handles historical lookups.

commonMistakes: Migrating ALL historical data including 3-year-old stale leads — pollutes grade distribution
Cancelling Zoho before day 90 — no fallback if something breaks
Treating migration as a consulting engagement — SMBs don't need one

faqs.q: Can we migrate custom fields?
faqs.a: Yes. Zoho custom fields export cleanly; Leadkaun supports custom fields in lead records.

faqs.q: What happens to Zoho workflows?
faqs.a: Workflows don't migrate directly. Map each workflow to Leadkaun's equivalent (Priority Queue, Missed Opportunity, Follow-up Engine) — most are covered natively.

faqs.q: What about Zoho Books / Desk integration?
faqs.a: Keep Zoho Books / Desk if you use them. Leadkaun focuses on sales behaviour; Books / Desk are separate concerns.

title: How to Calculate ₹ at Risk for Your Sales Pipeline
tldr: Formula: (avg deal value × grade conversion rate) summed over overdue leads. For 10-rep Indian B2B SMB: typically ₹2–5 lakh weekly baseline. Compute manually for 2 weeks; automate with Leadkaun past that.
why: ₹ at risk is the single metric that turns sales reviews from activity debates into money conversations. It makes abstract 'overdue follow-up' concrete. Reps work differently when they see their own number. Managers coach differently. Reviews become conversations about recovery.
prerequisites: 90 days of closed-won deal data
Grade system in place (even manual)
Defined staleness SLA per grade

steps.heading: Compute avg deal value per industry
steps.body: Pull last 90 days of closed-won. Sum deal values ÷ count. Example: real estate team sees avg GCV ₹45 lakh.

steps.heading: Compute conversion rate per grade
steps.body: For each grade, count leads that closed ÷ total leads at that grade over same 90 days. Example: Grade A = 10%, Grade B = 5%, Grade C = 2%.

steps.heading: Compute ₹ per stale lead by grade
steps.body: Multiply avg deal value × conversion rate. Grade A real estate: ₹45L × 10% = ₹4.5L per stale lead.

steps.heading: Count stale leads by grade (per rep)
steps.body: Past SLA: Grade A 24h, Grade B 48h, Grade C 7d. Count per rep.

steps.heading: Sum: ₹ at risk = Σ(count × ₹ per grade)
steps.body: Example: 4 Grade A stale × ₹4.5L = ₹18L. Plus 8 Grade B × ₹2.3L = ₹18.4L. Total rep: ₹36.4L.

steps.heading: Surface in Monday manager review
steps.body: Open stand-up: 'Team: ₹4.2L at risk. Priya: ₹1.8L, Rajesh: ₹1.2L, Mohan: ₹1.2L.' Discuss recovery plans per rep.

steps.heading: Surface in rep Morning Brief
steps.body: Rep version: '₹1.8L to recover today — top 3: [lead names]'. Frame as opportunity, not blame.

commonMistakes: Using best-case deal values instead of medians — inflates the number, reps tune out
Shaming reps publicly with the number — destroys adoption
Tracking only ₹ at risk without pairing with ₹ recovered

faqs.q: What's a healthy ₹ at risk number?
faqs.a: ₹2–5 lakh/week for a 10-rep Indian B2B SMB is healthy steady-state. First week often shows ₹8–15 lakh because backlog accumulated.

faqs.q: Does Leadkaun automate this?
faqs.a: Yes. Missed Opportunity Engine runs continuously; Morning Brief surfaces the number daily.

faqs.q: What if deal values vary wildly?
faqs.a: Use median, not mean. If still too variable, segment by product line and compute per segment.

title: How to Log WhatsApp Conversations for Sales Tracking
tldr: Use 3-tap logging after each meaningful WhatsApp exchange: tap stage (first-reply / asking-questions / objection / meeting-booked / ghosted), tap intent (high / medium / low / negative), tap outcome (progressed / stuck / closed). Takes 10 seconds, feeds Intent Score.
why: 70% of Indian B2B first-contact happens on WhatsApp. If you're not logging those exchanges as signals, your scoring model is missing half the data — and Grade A leads are being under-weighted. 3-tap logging is the minimum-viable capture that keeps reps in flow.
prerequisites: Lead exists in your system (imported or manually added)
WhatsApp conversation has substantive content (not just 'hi')
Rep has Leadkaun mobile web or app access

steps.heading: Identify the lead in Leadkaun
steps.body: Open the lead record (search by phone number — Leadkaun matches Indian formats automatically).

steps.heading: Tap 'Log WhatsApp'
steps.body: Opens the 3-tap UI. No typing required.

steps.heading: Tap stage
steps.body: Options: First reply, Asking questions, Negotiating, Objection, Meeting booked, Ghosted. Pick the current state of the conversation.

steps.heading: Tap intent
steps.body: Options: High (green), Medium (amber), Low (grey), Negative (red). Your read of where the conversation is heading.

steps.heading: Tap outcome
steps.body: Options: Progressed, Stuck, Closed. What happened in this specific exchange.

steps.heading: Optional: attach screenshot
steps.body: For important objections or quotes, attach a screenshot. Skip for routine logging.

steps.heading: Confirm
steps.body: Taps flow into Intent Score: High = +10, Medium = +5, Low = 0, Negative = −10. Priority Queue re-ranks if the lead crosses a grade threshold.

commonMistakes: Logging every WhatsApp 'hello' — only log meaningful exchanges
Using 'High' intent for everything — calibrate to actual conversation quality
Forgetting to log 'ghosted' — without it, the lead's grade won't decay correctly

faqs.q: Do I need WhatsApp Business API?
faqs.a: No. 3-tap logging works with regular WhatsApp — rep types in WhatsApp, logs in Leadkaun.

faqs.q: Does Leadkaun auto-detect WhatsApp replies?
faqs.a: Not for regular WhatsApp. With WhatsApp Business API + a BSP (Gupshup, AiSensy), auto-detection is on roadmap.

faqs.q: Should a manager see the logs?
faqs.a: Yes — per-rep WhatsApp activity shows on the Rep Performance card. Aggregate view only; no access to screenshots without permission.

title: How to Connect Google Sheets to Leadkaun (5-Min Sync)
tldr: Share your Google Sheet with the Leadkaun service account, paste the sheet URL into Leadkaun's connector, map columns (name, phone, email, source), set 5-minute polling. New rows flow into Leadkaun automatically.
why: Most Indian SMBs live in Google Sheets. Ad agencies dump lead data there weekly. WhatsApp enquiries get logged there manually. Instead of migrating, Leadkaun meets teams where they already work — the sheet becomes the live source, and Leadkaun polls every 5 minutes for new rows.
prerequisites: A Google Sheet with at least columns: name, phone, source
Permission to share the sheet
Leadkaun account with connector access

steps.heading: Prepare your sheet
steps.body: Row 1 = headers. Required columns: name, phone. Recommended: email, source, date-added, initial-enquiry, company.

steps.heading: Share with Leadkaun service account
steps.body: Leadkaun provides an email like imports@leadkaun.iam.gserviceaccount.com. Share the sheet with that address, Viewer permission.

steps.heading: Paste URL in Leadkaun connector
steps.body: In Leadkaun → Settings → Integrations → Google Sheets → Add sheet. Paste URL. Connector auto-detects headers.

steps.heading: Map columns
steps.body: Drag each sheet column to its Leadkaun field. Leadkaun pre-fills obvious matches (name, phone, email).

steps.heading: Set polling interval
steps.body: Default: 5 minutes. Faster polling costs API quota; 5 minutes is the sweet spot for most teams.

steps.heading: Test sync
steps.body: Add a test row to the sheet. Within 5 minutes it should appear as a new lead in Leadkaun, graded A–F automatically.

steps.heading: Monitor
steps.body: First week: check daily for duplicate leads (phone dedup should catch them) or mapping errors. After that, set-and-forget.

commonMistakes: Sharing sheet with Edit permission instead of Viewer — Leadkaun only needs read
Not including a unique identifier column (phone) — duplicates accumulate
Trying to sync >100k rows in one sheet — split into monthly sheets instead

faqs.q: Can I sync multiple sheets?
faqs.a: Yes — connect as many as you need. Each maps to a different source tag.

faqs.q: Does it delete rows?
faqs.a: No. Leadkaun reads only; deleting rows from the sheet doesn't remove leads from Leadkaun.

faqs.q: What if the sheet owner leaves?
faqs.a: Re-share with the Leadkaun service account from a new owner account. Existing data stays.

title: How to Train Reps on a New Sales Tool (Without Losing a Week)
tldr: 2-hour hands-on session per team, not a half-day slideshow. Reps log 5 real leads during the session. Follow up with a daily 5-minute check-in for the first week, then weekly.
why: Most 'sales tool rollouts' fail at week 2 because training was a PowerPoint, not a practice session. Reps leave knowing the features but not the workflow. Two weeks later, 30% have reverted to their old tool. Practice-based training closes the reversion gap.
prerequisites: Tool onboarding done (Leadkaun onboarded, leads imported)
One manager + one power-user rep as champions
Team available for 2 uninterrupted hours

steps.heading: Pre-session: import each rep's 10 real leads
steps.body: Training works on real data, not demo data. Each rep has 10 of their own leads loaded in Leadkaun.

steps.heading: Session 1 (30 min): tour the UI
steps.body: Manager walks through: Priority Queue, lead record, 3-tap logging, Morning Brief preview. No slides.

steps.heading: Session 2 (60 min): practice logging
steps.body: Each rep logs 5 real interactions (call, WhatsApp, meeting) live, using their own leads. Manager spot-checks the logs.

steps.heading: Session 3 (30 min): Q&A + manager coaching demo
steps.body: Show a sample Monday review: ₹ at risk per rep, recovery plans. Reps see what 'performance' looks like in the new tool.

steps.heading: Day 1 after: first Morning Brief
steps.body: 8:30 AM IST, every rep gets their Brief. Manager sends a WhatsApp message: 'You're on the queue. Work top-down. I'll check in at 11.'

steps.heading: Week 1: daily 5-min check-ins
steps.body: Every morning at 10 AM, manager asks each rep one question: 'Top of queue today?'. Keeps workflow front-of-mind.

steps.heading: Week 2 onwards: weekly review
steps.body: Monday stand-up uses ₹ at risk as the opening metric. 1:1s reference queue adoption rate.

commonMistakes: Long slide-deck training — retention is under 10% after 2 days
Using demo data — reps don't see how it applies to their own work
No manager follow-up in week 1 — adoption collapses

faqs.q: What if a senior rep pushes back?
faqs.a: Don't fight. Let them run their own order for a week. Measure. Data wins if the tool works.

faqs.q: How long until it feels natural?
faqs.a: Two weeks of daily use. Don't measure adoption before that — it's still the learning curve.

faqs.q: What if a rep never adopts?
faqs.a: After 60 days of support and coaching, it's a discipline problem, not a tool problem. Performance review territory.

title: How to Track Property Enquiries for Indian Real Estate Teams
tldr: Grade every enquiry A–F within 5 minutes of arrival, surface Grade A in the Priority Queue for immediate callback, target 47-minute response window. Log WhatsApp + call outcomes in 3 taps. Track site visit conversion per source.
why: Real estate is the most response-time-sensitive vertical in Indian B2B — Grade A enquiries contacted within 47 minutes convert 5× better than 4-hour callbacks. A single missed site visit is ₹5–50 lakh in lost GCV. The workflow is simple; the discipline is what wins.
prerequisites: Active lead sources (99acres, MagicBricks, Housing.com, Facebook Ads, walk-ins)
Per-project ICP (luxury vs affordable, 2BHK vs 3BHK segments)
Team of 3+ reps assigned to enquiries

steps.heading: Ingest from every source
steps.body: 99acres + MagicBricks → export to Google Sheet → Leadkaun polls every 5 min. FB Ads → CSV upload. Walk-ins → manual entry (15 seconds).

steps.heading: Auto-grade on arrival
steps.body: Score on Fit (project match + location match + budget fit) + Intent (source baseline) + Quality (phone/email/inquiry). Grade A if all three strong.

steps.heading: Surface Grade A for 47-minute callback
steps.body: Priority Queue + instant alert to assigned rep. Rep aims to make first contact within 47 minutes of enquiry.

steps.heading: Log every interaction in 3 taps
steps.body: Call outcome (interested / site visit booked / cold / wrong number). WhatsApp stage if engaged there. Site visit confirmation in one tap.

steps.heading: Follow-up cadence
steps.body: Grade A: 3 touches in day 1 (call, WA, call). Grade B: call + WA week 1. Grade C: weekly WA nurture.

steps.heading: Track conversion by source
steps.body: Source Truth card shows ₹ closed per source over last 90 days. Kill bad sources fast; double down on good ones.

steps.heading: Manager Monday review
steps.body: ₹ at risk per rep. Site visit conversion by source. Recovery plans for Grade A leads past SLA.

commonMistakes: Not normalising phone numbers — 20% duplicate leads from same enquirer
Calling freshest lead first instead of Grade A first
Losing WhatsApp thread when the rep changes shifts — always log the stage

faqs.q: What's the ideal response time?
faqs.a: 47 minutes for Grade A enquiries. 4–6 hours for Grade B. Data from Indian real estate teams shows 5× conversion when hit in window.

faqs.q: Can broker splits be tracked?
faqs.a: Yes — source attribution with commission splits configurable.

faqs.q: Does it integrate with 99acres directly?
faqs.a: Not natively in Phase 1 — Google Sheets export is the bridge. Native 99acres API on roadmap.

title: How to Score Student Enquiries for EdTech Admissions
tldr: Weight Fit heavily on course-match + programme-match. Intent decays faster during admissions season. Log WhatsApp parent replies in 3 taps — 80% of admissions conversations happen there. Target 2-hour response window for Grade A.
why: EdTech admissions is seasonal, WhatsApp-heavy, and parent-decision-driven. Counsellors carry 200–500 leads each — without a scoring system, 30 minutes of morning triage is burned before any calls happen. The right scoring model is the difference between 12 more admissions or 0 more admissions in a given cycle.
prerequisites: Course catalogue mapped (programme, fees, cohort start dates)
Admissions cycle calendar defined
Parent + student as primary + secondary contacts on lead records

steps.heading: Define course-specific ICP
steps.body: For each programme, weight Fit: course match (40), programme match (20), geography (15), parent engagement (15), budget signal (10).

steps.heading: Set admissions-cycle-aware Intent decay
steps.body: In-season (March–June): −2/day decay. Off-season: −0.5/day decay. Different cadence for different programmes.

steps.heading: Capture parent + student separately
steps.body: Lead record has primary contact (parent) and secondary (student). Either can engage on WhatsApp; both feed Intent.

steps.heading: Instant Grade A alert to counsellor
steps.body: New Grade A enquiry → push notification to assigned counsellor within 60 seconds. 2-hour SLA for first call.

steps.heading: 3-tap WhatsApp logging
steps.body: Parent replies: tap stage + intent + outcome. Feeds Intent Score. Threaded history visible to counsellor.

steps.heading: Course-completion follow-up cadence
steps.body: Grade A: counsellor call day 1, WhatsApp day 2, parent call day 4. Grade B: weekly nurture with course content.

steps.heading: Morning Brief per counsellor
steps.body: 8:30 AM IST email: '8 Grade A enquiries replied overnight. ₹6L in admissions at risk if not called by 11 AM.'

commonMistakes: Same scoring across programmes — a ₹5L MBA is not a ₹30k test prep
Not decaying Intent during cycle changes — June's Grade A is worthless in August
Counsellor cherry-picking instead of working queue top-down

faqs.q: Can we set different ICP per programme?
faqs.a: Yes on Growth tier. Engineering admissions, arts programmes, and PG programmes each get their own weights.

faqs.q: What about multi-course enquiries?
faqs.a: Lead record supports multiple product-interests. Primary interest drives the main grade.

faqs.q: What if a counsellor leaves mid-cycle?
faqs.a: One-click handover: lead record, history, WhatsApp thread, all transfer to new counsellor.

title: How to Run a Weekly Sales Review That Drives Recovery
tldr: 30-minute Monday review anchored on ₹ at risk and ₹ recovered — not calls made or meetings booked. Per-rep rollup opens the discussion. Each rep walks out with a specific ₹ recovery target for the week.
why: Most sales reviews are activity theatre — 'how many calls did you make?' — which doesn't move revenue. A review that starts with ₹ at risk per rep and ends with specific recovery plans shifts the entire team's incentives. Reps start owning outcomes, not just activity.
prerequisites: ₹ at risk metric running (manual or via Leadkaun Missed Opportunity Engine)
Grade system in place
30-minute Monday block with all reps + manager

steps.heading: Open with team ₹ at risk
steps.body: First line: 'Team ₹ at risk this week: ₹4.2 lakh. Last week: ₹3.1 lakh. Up or down — why?'

steps.heading: Per-rep rollup (3 min each)
steps.body: Each rep: their ₹ at risk, their ₹ recovered last week, top 3 stale leads they're targeting this week.

steps.heading: Identify stuck leads
steps.body: Any lead past Grade A SLA and uncontacted 3+ times goes into 'escalation' — manager takes over or reassigns.

steps.heading: Source-level pattern check (3 min)
steps.body: Any source with >30% junk or >50% stale? Flag for sales-marketing conversation.

steps.heading: Individual coaching moment (5 min)
steps.body: Pick one rep whose numbers shifted most (up or down). 5-minute public-facing coaching conversation.

steps.heading: Commitment round (5 min)
steps.body: Each rep states: '₹X I'll recover this week from [specific leads].' Write it down. Reference next Monday.

steps.heading: Close with Morning Brief reminder
steps.body: 'Your Brief hits at 8:30 tomorrow. Top of queue by 10 AM. See you Friday.'

commonMistakes: Opening with call volume — sets wrong frame for the whole meeting
Running over 30 minutes — reps tune out, lose the ritual
No public commitment round — nothing to follow up on next week

faqs.q: Monday or Friday?
faqs.a: Monday. Sets the week; doesn't just recap it.

faqs.q: What if a rep's ₹ at risk is high every week?
faqs.a: Diagnose: discipline issue (skip-queue), source issue (bad lead quality), or capacity issue (too many leads per rep)? Different fixes.

faqs.q: How long until culture shifts?
faqs.a: 8–12 weeks of consistent rhythm. Slower if reps resist; faster if leadership is visible in the ritual.
